Subject: Creativity Is Our Mission - Here's Why
Body: Creativity Is Our Mission - Here's Why Hello Again, Honey Potter! We wanted to share a little bit more about our mission at A Beautiful Mess because the truth is, it's about you. We want you to know that you are creative. Period. You have many beautiful and fantastic things you ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ Hello Again, Honey Potter! We wanted to share a little bit more about our mission at A Beautiful Mess because the truth is, it's about you. We want you to know that you are creative. Period. You have many beautiful and fantastic things you are going to make in your life. Some you might share with the world, and some you might keep just for you. You have everything you need already to make these things. You don't need to wait until you have more time, talent, money, or whatever excuse might pop into your head. You can make stuff right now! We believe that making things is the secret to a good life. And we want you to have a great life! We want to live a great life for ourselves, too. So, let's make stuff!!!! Consider us your #1 creativity cheerleaders from now on. Do you remember the three ways we shared in the last email for how you can keep up with us? It was to read our blog, listen to our podcast, and follow us on Instagram. Our goal on each of these platforms (as well as this newsletter you're reading right now) is to add a little positivity to your day, make you smile, and remind you to make stuff. If you haven't heard it from anyone else in your life we hope you hear this from us often: YOU ARE CREATIVE. xo. Elsie & Emma 731 S. Pickwick AvenueSpringfield, MO 65802, United States Unsubscribe or Manage Preferences

Subject: Cameron: 'You can get it if you really want'
Body: Cameron: 'You can get it if you really want' ========================================= The man who would be prime minister in a month or so if an electon is called, and if he wins, has spoken. David Cameron carried off that public school accomplishment of speaking without hesitation, deviation or repetition - and without a script. Remarkable enough in front of an audience reduced from Mrs Thatcher's 3,000 in Blackpool to 1,200. And he is unashamed to say how much he admires Eton, although he didn't name it - he just said it was a fantastic school. He also revealed himself to be a Jimmy Cliff fan, one of the few areas of repetition involving the following famous phrase, 'You can get it if you really want.' Cathy Newman is in Blackpool, savouring the aftermath of the Cameron speech and assessing whether he was wise to call on Mr Brown to have an immediate election - doesn't that make it harder to attack him for needlessly calling an election when he does? We'll be assessing where we are regarding election campaigns, tensions, possibilities and more with Ken Clarke, Vince Cable and David Blunkett. Not just the ï¿½will he, wonï¿½t he?ï¿½ debate, but the ï¿½should he, shouldnï¿½t he?ï¿½ - which is much more interesting. Cameron fights for his political life: http://tinyurl.com/ynvpe3 'Cameron's speech reduced' - by Hack: http://tinyurl.com/yozrde Diana inquest latest ================ The Diana inquest rolls on and new footage and photos of the scene of her end are revealed. Jane Dodge is down there, observing the coming and goings, not the least of which is Harrods owner muttering that the Royal Family murdered his son Dodi. But it must be admitted he's done pretty well to get the inquiry even as far as this - even if in the end, as the judge put it, there was little doubt that the driver of the death car had indeed drunk alcohol. Watch our earlier report: http://tinyurl.com/ywooyf Burma: new pictures ================ Talking of new video, there is new footage out of Burma and it's pretty horrid and pretty revealing. Jonathan Miller is looking at it and reporting on it from Bangkok. Burma latest: http://tinyurl.com/2djaky Sex traffickers targeted ===================== New figures reveal the horrific scale of the trafficking in women, particularly those from the erstwhile Eastern Block. Once again, the spotlight fails to fall on the men who exploit them although police have today launched a new initiative to try to track the trafficking gangs. Sex traffic criminals targeted: http://tinyurl.com/24k6t4 Bye bye Blackpool =============== Finally, we return to Blackppool to say goodbye. Alas the other centres of population in Britain have so spruced up their conference capacity that Blackpool is falling by the wayside. And having lost the opportunity to sully its fine reputation with a giant casino, it is now losing the party conferences as well. Nicholas Glass reports on one of the great political venues of our time. Best wishes Jon Snow Channel 4 News services ==================== Online: Watch our video reports at: http://www.channel4.com/news/watchlisten/video/ Subscribe to RSS feeds, podcasts and mobile phone bulletins. http://www.channel4.com/news/subscribe/ Business ====== At the time of sending the FTSE-100 index was up 34.8 points at 6535.2 The US Dollar to Sterling was: 2.03 The Euro to Sterling was: 1.44 Weather ===== Sponsored by Worcester, Bosch Group - www.worcester-bosch.co.uk Tonight Rain across western Scotland will move east, but showers will persist in the west. Rain over Wales will move east and die out. Dry elsewhere with clear intervals. Thursday Cloudy across Scotland with outbreaks of rain, but becoming drier later. Mainly dry elsewhere with bright or sunny spells but isolated showers may develop in the north. http://www.channel4.com/weather Unsubscribe ======== http://www.channel4.com/news/snowmail/snowmail_deactivate.html If you want to reply to Snowmail please email ckns@channel4.com and put Snowmail in the subject header. DO NOT hit reply to this email as they cannot be read. Thank you.

Subject: Will renters rise up in Toronto’s election?
Body: First Up Plus: Who gets the final say on a listing price? ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ LIVING HERE By Amber Shortt Good morning. Here’s the latest on cottage country’s new normal, how renters could shape the Toronto election and who should get final say on a listing price. GOOD WEEK FOR... Homebuilders. A jump in sales shows buyers are trickling back to the GTA’s newly built home market. But, as BILD’s CEO says, now is not the time to call the market “anywhere near being balanced” — we still need more housing. Homebuyers. Bidding wars are back — but if you have the means and a strong stomach, the Star’s David Olive says it’s a good time to get into the market. Here's why this time is different. Dockside deskers. Elon Musk may still have a few fans, as hybrid workers in Ontario’s cottage country hail his satellite internet service as a “game changer.” It’s one factor in cottage country’s new normal. Where should you eat next? If you’re wondering where to eat as the city heats up, you need Food Crawl, the Star’s free food newsletter. Whether it’s a strip mall standout or a downtown gem, Food Crawl will make you an insider on the best eats in Toronto and beyond. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... Caledon residents, who are caught in the middle of the Peel Region divorce. It’s meant to help speed up housing, but sorting out the details will likely be a headache. Indebted Canadians. Canadians have racked up more household debt than any other G7 country, with about three-quarters stemming from mortgages — and it will likely get worse. Young investors. Not surprisingly, a new Statistics Canada report shows the real estate investment market is dominated by boomers, with the number of homes investor-owned at 15 per cent. Here’s who else is investing in Canadian real estate. TRENDING During the last municipal election, a candidate knocked on my door and told my partner not to sell our house, listing all the reasons property values in our former city of York neighbourhood would be rising. And when my partner told him we were renting? “Oh,” he said. “That’s too bad.” I’d say I can’t blame him for being surprised. To most people, the idea of a renter conjures up an image of an undergrad student in a basement eating Kraft Dinner — not a family of four in a detached house, well, also eating Kraft Dinner (parents of three-year-olds, you get it). But not only is the number of renting households increasing, it’s also becoming more diverse — meaning renters could be a force in next month’s mayoral election. “It’s everyone — young professionals, families, seniors — and they’re all struggling because Toronto is not an affordable city to live in,” tenant advocate Douglas Kwan told the Star’s Victoria Gibson and May Warren this week. Tenants’ frustration is mounting over rising rents, threats of eviction and, for some, the lost dream of home ownership as prices rise again. Mayoral hopefuls this time around are waking up to the notion that the 48 per cent of Toronto households who pay rent instead of a mortgage could decide their fate. But as policies to increase housing affordability roll off candidates’ tongues, the big question may not be whether renters vote, but will they vote together? As University of Toronto professor Matti Siemiatycki told the Star, it’s not a foregone conclusion that renters will vote as a block, saying, “Whether it’s urban and suburban, or motorist and cyclist and pedestrian, there are all sorts of other dynamics that people see as important to their identity and interests.” Still, as the housing crisis deepens, any candidate who ignores renters’ concerns may just find tenants have no interest in giving them a four-year lease on the mayor’s seat. And that’s too bad. LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND “Drive until you qualify” is pushing home hunters farther from the city and to places like Bradford, Ont., where this home doubled in price in a decade. The Residences of the Well at Front St. and Spadina Ave. can only be rented, not bought by investors. One renter, life coach Katia Stern, shares why it works for her. This Richmond Street East building now boasts Canada’s first residential air-to-water heat pump — technology that could make all condos carbon-free. Check it out. Remember that Kensington laneway home with a rooftop shower? It’s still for sale for a slightly lower asking price of just under $3 million. Soak it in. [Realtor.ca] Whitby’s heritage committee decided a home built in the late 1800s, with no discernible historical value, can be torn down for a new housing complex. [DurhamRegion.com] In Vienna’s Gemeindebauten (social housing), the proportion of tenants’ incomes that goes to rent is in the single digits. What can Toronto learn? [The New York Times] SHOULD I OVERRULE MY AGENT ON LIST PRICE? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS The Star talked to Property.ca broker Julian Kashani about how agents decide on a list price, and what happens when an agent and seller disagree. Here’s what he said: When suggesting a price to a client, I usually do a comparative market analysis to get a sense of what similar properties have recently sold for. I even take my client to other currently listed properties in their area and show them the pluses and minuses of a property compared to their own. If we do disagree on price, I advise we price it a little below what we think the property is worth but do an offer date — only accepting offers on one day — and see what the market will offer. This sales strategy is often done in hot sellers’ markets and when it is hard to price a property because it is unique. If the client wants to list at a higher price, thinking we can lower it later if needed, I explain they risk staying in the market too long, which could lead buyers to wonder if there’s something wrong with the property. An agent’s job is to give their client the best possible information, but at the end of the day it’s their property and the price is their choice. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Before you go, here’s a look inside the moated community of Toronto Islands. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week. Get unlimited access to the Star Limited time sale: Only $1/week for 52 weeks Get This Offer If you're not enjoying these emails, please tell us how we can make them better by emailing newsletterfeedback@thestar.ca. Or, if you'd prefer, you can unsubscribe from this newsletter by clicking the first link below. One Yonge Street, 4th Floor, Toronto, ON M5E 1E6. 416-367-2000 PRIVACY POLICY

Subject: [UAI] KI2004: 2nd Call For Participation
Body: =================================================================== 27th GERMAN CONFERENCE ON ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE -- KI2004 -- =================================================================== (co-located with INFORMATIK 2004) September 20-24, Ulm, Germany http://ki2004.uni-ulm.de/ ************* EARLY REGISTRATION DEADLINE AUGUST 20th ************* CALL FOR PARTICIPATION KI-2004 cordially invites AI researchers and practitioners to meet in the beautiful town of Ulm on the banks of the Danube in September this year. An attractive technical programme has been put together, which covers a particularly broad spectrum of subfields in AI theory and practice. It comprises paper and poster presentations, a variety of specialised workshops and is highlighted by a number of invited talks from the areas of Automated Reasoning (JÃ¶rg Siekmann), Robot Planning (Malik Ghallab), Constraint Reasoning (FranÃ§ois Fages), Machine Learning (Martin Riedmiller), and Semantic Web (Wolfgang Wahlster). KI-2004 is co-located with INFORMATIK 2004, the German Conference on Informatics. KI and INFORMATIK share a joint day of invited presentations. In addition to the talks by Wolfgang Wahlster and Malik Ghallab, which are part of this joint programme, various speakers from academia and industry will give keynotes on their visions in areas like Ubiquitous Computing, Web Services, and Business Process Management. We look forward to an exciting and inspiring conference and to welcome you in Ulm shortly. KI-2004 organisation committee

Subject: BBC(Bleadperl Breaks CPAN) Today: SQL::Translator (0.08001)
Body: Hi Jess, if you haven't yet seen previous BBC articles, the intent is to bring the bleeding edge perl people together with those whose modules on CPAN suffer from expected changes in the next version of perl. You may not be amused by the fact that your module has an issue with bleadperl, but chances are good that the problem can be resolved before 5.10 comes out if only somebody chimes in to explain what's the issue. See the facts. Somehow your code triggers a different behaviour of perl since patch 26487 (http://public.activestate.com/cgi-bin/perlbrowse/p/26487) Change 26487 by nicholas@nicholas-saigo on 2005/12/26 12:31:01 Rework constant.pm to take advantage of the space savings of proxy constant subroutines whenever it can. which leads to a test failure: t/37-translator-graph......Can't locate object method "is_trivial_link" via package "GLOB" at /home/sand/.cpan/build/SQL-Translator-0.08001-oWjySe/blib/lib/SQL/Translator/Schema/Graph.pm line 46. # Looks like you planned 3 tests but only ran 2. # Looks like your test died just after 2. dubious Test returned status 255 (wstat 65280, 0xff00) DIED. FAILED test 3 Failed 1/3 tests, 66.67% okay I had no success when I tried to reduce the bug to a small testcase, sorry. Maybe Nicholas will follow up with an explanation what is happening? Hope this helps, -- andreas (hoping that this is the last BBC article before 5.10 comes out)
text:
BBC(Bleadperl Breaks CPAN) Today: SQL::Translator (0.08001)

Hi Jess, if you haven't yet seen previous BBC articles, the intent is to bring the bleeding edge perl people together with those whose modules on CPAN suffer from expected changes in the next version of perl. You may not be amused by the fact that your module has an issue with bleadperl, but chances are good that the problem can be resolved before 5.10 comes out if only somebody chimes in to explain what's the issue. See the facts. Somehow your code triggers a different behaviour of perl since patch 26487 (http://public.activestate.com/cgi-bin/perlbrowse/p/26487) Change 26487 by nicholas@nicholas-saigo on 2005/12/26 12:31:01 Rework constant.pm to take advantage of the space savings of proxy constant subroutines whenever it can. which leads to a test failure: t/37-translator-graph......Can't locate object method "is_trivial_link" via package "GLOB" at /home/sand/.cpan/build/SQL-Translator-0.08001-oWjySe/blib/lib/SQL/Translator/Schema/Graph.pm line 46. # Looks like you planned 3 tests but only ran 2. # Looks like your test died just after 2. dubious Test returned status 255 (wstat 65280, 0xff00) DIED. FAILED test 3 Failed 1/3 tests, 66.67% okay I had no success when I tried to reduce the bug to a small testcase, sorry. Maybe Nicholas will follow up with an explanation what is happening? Hope this helps, -- andreas (hoping that this is the last BBC article before 5.10 comes out)

Account Security Lock
